It is formally shown in this section that unidentifiability of the BLIM due to forward or backward gradedness of dichotomous knowledge structures (e.g.,  Spoto et al., 2012, 2013; Stefanutti & Spoto, 2020; Stefanutti et al., 2018 ) is a special case of unidentifiablity of the PoLIM with graded polytomous structures.

There is a clear connection between  Definition 2  and the notions of BG and FG defined for the dichotomous knowledge structures. By setting  
                         
                            V 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               , 
                               1 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , every knowledge state  
                         
                            K 
                            : 
                            Q 
                            → 
                            V 
                         
                       can be translated into the corresponding set representation. Given a polytomous structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            V 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      , with  
                         
                            V 
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               , 
                               1 
                               } 
                            
                         
                      , let  
                         
                            d 
                            : 
                            K 
                            → 
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                       be such that, for every  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                      , and every  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      :  
                         
                            
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  d 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     K

                                     q

Therefore, in the dichotomous case, gradedness takes always the form of either forward or backward gradedness. We have seen in Section  4  that this is not the case with more than two response categories.

We now show that  Definition 4  is, indeed, equivalent to Definition 2 in  Stefanutti et al. (2018)  whenever the dichotomous structure is BG, or to Definition 3 in the same article if it is FG. This last case is provided by  Definition 1  in this article. According to  Definition 2  in this article, the structure is FG in  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       whenever  
                         
                            a 
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            b 
                            = 
                            1 
                         
                      , and it is BG in the opposite case. Consequently,

                               a 
                               b 
                            
                         
                       coincides with the backward transformation  
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       defined in  Stefanutti et al. (2018)  when  
                         
                            a 
                            = 
                            1 
                         
                      , whereas it coincides with the forward transformation  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       when  
                         
                            a 
                            = 
                            0 
                         
                      .

                            Which is known to hold true by Eq.  (1) . □

Similar results could be obtained for the BG case ( Spoto et al., 2013 ). In conclusion,  Theorems 8 and 9  together show that the identifiability issues arising with dichotomous items, which are due to FG or BG, are special cases of those that arise in the polytomous case because of gradedness.

In this article we first studied the deterministic properties of graded polytomous knowledge structures. Moreover, as shown in Section  7 , we verified that the well-established and widely studied problem of unidentifiability of the BLIM when applied to FG and BG dichotomous knowledge structures, is a special case of the unidentifiability of the PoLIM when applied to graded polytomous structures.

Interestingly enough, we found that whenever a structure is graded in an item, gradedness is transitive and reflexive, and thus, it is a quasi-order on the set  
                         V 
                       of levels. Another very interesting deterministic property that was highlighted in this paper is the generalization of the concept of equally informative items in dichotomous structures, which turns out to be a special case of the more general concept of equivalent items that can be observed in polytomous structures. As happens in the dichotomous case for equally informative items, the presence of equivalent items in a polytomous structure excludes the possibility that the structure is graded in any of them. These results pave the way for the study of further possible deterministic properties of graded polytomous structures.

The main result of the present article is the definition of the outcome preserving transformations that map different PoLIM instances to the same distribution on the response patterns. What is really interesting is that such transformations represent, once again, the generalization of the forward and backward transformations defined for the dichotomous structures. Nevertheless, as for the case of equivalent items, some specific characteristics of the polytomous case emerged in the definition of the interval of values within which the outcome-preserving transformations are inner. In particular, the two collections  
                         
                            
                               W 
                            
                            
                               a 
                               b 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               Z 
                            
                            
                               a 
                               b 
                            
                         
                       can be both nonempty only in the polytomous case, while in the dichotomous one it has been shown that the former is necessarily always empty.

The proposed numerical example should help the reader to grasp all the involved passages and to show how the parameters of the PoLIM are modified by the transformations.

In this article the problem of the identifiability of the PoLIM was tackled from the perspective of the outcome preserving transformations. There is an alternative route, that most frequently is chosen for studying identifiability. This approach analyses the Jacobian matrix of the prediction function of the model ( Doignon, Heller, & Stefanutti, 2018; Heller, 2017 ). If the Jacobian matrix is full rank at a given point of the parameter space, then the model is locally identifiable at that point. The two theoretical approaches lead to equivalent results in the dichotomous case, as shown in  Stefanutti et al. (2018) . The Jacobian matrix method was not pursued in this article, but it could be the subject matter of future research.

We must stress that the present results apply only to the case of unidentifiability due to gradedness of the deterministic structure and that there could be other sources of unidentifiability beyond gradedness. For example, in the dichotomous case it was shown that unidentifiability of the BLIM also arises with the so-called “potentially (forward- or backward-) graded” structures ( Stefanutti & Spoto, 2021 ). These are structures that are “almost” graded – to which forward- and backward- transformations cannot be applied – but still unidentifiable. A similar situation is highly plausible in the polytomous case. Analogous considerations can be made for the concept of “empirically indistinguishable” knowledge structures ( Stefanutti & Spoto, 2021 ) that, in the dichotomous case, is closely related to the concept of potentially graded structures. All of these issues deserve future investigation.

The theory of identifiability of the BLIM was recently applied to the study of identifiability of probabilistic models in the dichotomous item response theory (e.g., the 3PL and 4PL models;  Noventa et al., 2019; Noventa, Ye, Kelava, & Spoto, 2024 ). Even here it is interesting to investigate if the theoretical results on the identifiability of the PoLIM can be transferred to polytomous item response theory models.

Finally, concerning competence based knowledge space theory (see, e.g.,  Doignon, 1994; Heller, Ünlü, & Albert, 2013 ) the study of the identifiability of a competence based version of the BLIM, named CBLIM ( Heller, Stefanutti, Anselmi, & Robusto, 2015 ), was carried out by  Spoto and Stefanutti (2020, 2023) . Here the relationship between items and dichotomous skills is modeled in various ways. In particular, necessary and sufficient conditions under which such models give rise to FG or BG knowledge structures were established ( Spoto & Stefanutti, 2020 ). The competence-based approach was recently extended to politomous items ( Stefanutti, Spoto, Anselmi, & de Chiusole, 2023 ), giving rise to a more complex and composite situation for which, currently, a theory of identifiability is still missing.
